## Why This Is Asked

```c
#ifndef STATS_H
#define STATS_H
void compute_stats(const double* data, size_t n, double* mean, double* stddev);
#endif
```

```go
package cgobind

/*
#include "stats.h"
*/
import "C"
import (
  "unsafe"
)

func ComputeStats(data []float64) (mean, stddev float64) {
  if len(data) == 0 {
    return 0, 0
  }
  var cMean, cStddev C.double
  ptr := (*C.double)(unsafe.Pointer(&data[0]))
  C.compute_stats(ptr, C.size_t(len(data)), &cMean, &cStddev)
  return float64(cMean), float64(cStddev)
}
```

// binding.go

Bridging C callbacks into Go via CGO is error-prone: Go pointers cannot be passed into C, callbacks require a stable Go function and a safe handle, and concurrency multiplies lifetime questions.

## Key Concepts
- CGO callback bridging
- Go pointer safety and handles
- Memory ownership across FFI
- Per-stream concurrency and error propagation

## Code Example
```go

import "C"
// ...
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you test the wrapper under multiple producers?
- How would you handle backpressure and error propagation in callbacks?
